verdict_reasoning: FN1 (fibronectin) is a large secreted glycoprotein, but it has a well-documented cell-surface form: assembled fibronectin fibrils are anchored to cells via integrin (α5β1, αvβ3) binding through the RGD motif, and FN1 is incorporated into pericellular matrix. Critically, alternative splicing produces oncofetal isoforms (EDA/EDB-containing, e.g., FN1-EDB) that are deposited in tumor vasculature/stroma and are clinical targets of antibodies, ADCs, and radioligands (e.g., L19-based therapeutics targeting EDB). Surface/matrix deposition is wash-resistant via covalent disulfide cross-linking during fibrillogenesis and tissue transglutaminase crosslinking. This fits stable_surface_attachment via TM-partner (integrin) anchoring plus matrix incorporation in specific tissue/disease contexts.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Borderline between secreted_only (matrix/stromal deposition explicitly excluded per spec) and stable_surface_attachment via integrin tethering; clinical anti-EDB programs target matrix-deposited form.
